dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 60 for a traumatic injury call at 900 Hermitage Rd in [CITY] with [COMPANY_NAME] units on scene. [COMPANY_NAME] officers will deliver the patient to the unit en route.
Hello, I'm Alex, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What caused you to call 911 today?
I was struck in the right knee with something sharp, maybe a brick or asphalt, during an incident. I'm in constant 5 out of 10 pain, can't bear weight on it, and the area is very tender and swollen.
Tell me your name, what happened, your current location, and today's date?
Thomas, you're alert and oriented to name, event, location, and date. Good job.
Airway is patent with no secretions. You are speaking clearly.
Breathing is not labored, and your lung sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
Your pulse is 129. It's elevated but steady.
18 breaths per minute. Not labored.
Blood pressure is 142 over 102. Let's continue the assessment.
Your glucose is 135. Stable for now.
Oxygen saturation is 100%. Good sign of adequate breathing.
Let me examine your knee further. Can I take a closer look at your right knee?
The skin is warm to touch, normal in color for your ethnicity, with swelling and some crepitus upon palpation.
I noted a deformity to your right knee and obvious pain upon manipulation.
Pulse is present, strong, and regular in all extremities.
GCS is 15. You're alert, with equal and reactive pupils-looks good on the neurological side.
No tracheal deviation or JVD. Breathing is still unlabored.
When did you notice the injury?
Did the injury occur when you were struck or upon trying to move?
How would you describe the pain-sharp, dull, or throbbing? Anything along the lines of clicking or popping?
Thomas, do you have any medical conditions, medications currently, or known allergies?

I have ADHD and take Adderall occasionally. I have a shellfish allergy. No other drug allergies or current medications. The injury occurred when I was struck on the right knee by the point of what I think was a brick or piece of asphalt. I didn't hear or feel anything snap or pop.
Blood pressure is now 163 over 100. It's still high.
17 breaths per minute. Still unlabored.
Oxygen saturation is now 96%. You're maintaining good oxygen levels.
We are applying a general pillow splint to your right lower leg to stabilize the injury. Okay with that?
We placed an 18-gauge IV in your left arm as a saline lock. I'll flush it now. Any issues?
We are placing 4-lead EKG monitoring as a precaution due to tachycardia. Ready for the leads?
[COMPANY_NAME] is the requested facility. They have the resources for your care.
Thomas, we'll secure you on the stretcher. Scoot up slowly and let us support your leg. Seat belt and harness now-everything secure.
Retaking your current vitals now, as is standard when preparing for transport.
I noted no deformity in the rest of your leg or body. Is your skin anywhere else broken or swollen?
Still patent airway. No change.
There is no deformity noted in your left leg, arms, or back. Any concern with those areas?
Pain is still 5 out of 10. Any need for pain medication while we transport?
Still not in respiratory distress.
No rashes, abrasions, or wounds noted elsewhere. Just the knee swollen and tender.
Pupils remain round and reactive to light. No neurological deficits.
No new medications in the last 24 hours, correct?
Blood pressure is still persistently high at 163 over 100.
Oxygen saturation is still at 96%.
Pulse remains strong and regular at all sites.
No pain upon palpation to upper or lower back.
Thomas, you were struck while on duty, correct?
Skin appears normal with no signs of irritation or damage in other areas.
Your neck appears normal, no rigidity or signs of fracture.
No bleeding from ears or nose. Oral mucosa pink and moist.
Breath sounds still clear and equal bilaterally.
Chest appears normal with no asymmetry or abnormal movement.
No tenderness or rigidity in the abdomen.
Any vomiting or dizziness recently before the injury?
Any abdominal pain or incontinence?
Oxygen saturation still remains at 96%.
Glucose read is stable at 135.
You do confirm you do not feel or hear anything popping at the time of injury or during movement, correct?
And you were on duty when this happened, correct?
A crew member is holding your right knee for support during movement.
No jugular venous distention.
Breathing is 17. Still unlabored.
Pulse is still 125-so it's trending slightly lower.
[COMPANY_NAME] will be taking over your care shortly.
Transfer to stretcher is stable with upright positioning and safety harness.
I'll test extra-ocular movements. Please follow my finger.
No signs of gaze preference or nystagmus.
You can move your left leg freely and without pain.
Any new onset of symptoms since the incident?
No issues during staging of the transport.
RAA crew members assist in stabilization during transfer to the ED bed.
We initiated Protocol 4-1: Injury-General Trauma Management.
Performing routine vital checks and monitoring.
Continuous monitoring of EKG and pulse remains in place.
Re-admission vitals were taken again at 02:35. All readings remain stable.
IV line appears secure, and EKG is functioning well.
Pain is still at 5 out of 10, localized to the right knee.
No changes in condition-status remains stable.
Disarmed by [COMPANY_NAME] as a precaution for potentially sedative procedures.
No signs of drowsiness or distress noted.
You maintained mental status throughout the entire trip.
No deterioration or complications occurred during transport.
You came in as [COMPANY_NAME] officers delivered you-no delays in transport.
No detour or changes in rating en route.
Spo2 holding at 96% as noted.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia in both assessments.
You remain in constant communication without distress.
ED staff were informed via radio contact about tachycardia and non-life-threatening status.
You were triaged as stable both en route and upon arrival.
No pre-transport sedation was administered due to lack of distress.
You were placed in bed in the ED with no incident.
Right leg was stabilized by RAA crew during transfer with no complaints.
No issues were raised from the handed-off report or patient belongings.
Your personal items were kept with you throughout transport for easy retrieval.
No delays, no critical interventions in between.
We note from your provided information that you have ADHD and shellfish allergy.
